title: British Literature
intro: British Literature questions draw most heavily on the Victorian and modernist canons, with Thomas Hardy, George Bernard Shaw, Harold Pinter, John Keats, John Donne, Gerard Manley Hopkins, Virginia Woolf, and Charles Dickens forming the most frequently asked answerlines. Poetry is tested across every era, from Old English alliterative verse and Chaucer's Middle English through the Metaphysical poets, the Romantics (Wordsworth, Coleridge, Blake, Byron, Shelley, Keats), and the Victorians (Tennyson, Browning, Arnold) to twentieth-century figures such as Yeats, Eliot, Auden, Larkin, and Heaney.

Drama clusters around two poles: Shakespeare and his Elizabethan-Jacobean contemporaries (Marlowe, Jonson, Webster, Middleton) on one end, and twentieth-century British and Irish playwrights — Shaw, Wilde, Beckett, Pinter, and Stoppard, largely under the banners of the Theatre of the Absurd and Angry Young Men — on the other. The novel is dominated by Victorian realists (Dickens, Eliot, the Brontes, Thackeray, Hardy) and by modernists and their heirs (Joyce, Woolf, Forster, Conrad, Lawrence), with a substantial contemporary tail of Booker-adjacent and postcolonial writers including Ishiguro, Rushdie, Zadie Smith, Atwood, and Mantel.

Ireland supplies an outsized share of the canon across every era — Swift and Sheridan in the eighteenth century, Wilde in the fin de siecle, Yeats, Joyce, and Beckett in modernism, and Heaney among Nobel laureates — reflecting the shared literary history bundled under "British Literature." Scotland (Burns, Scott, Muriel Spark) and Wales (Dylan Thomas) contribute smaller but recurring clusters. A long tail of generic motifs (gardens, the sea, swans, dreams) and non-British comparanda (Dante, Virgil, Moliere, Homer, the Greek and Roman classics) also recurs, usually as source material, analogues, or common-link scaffolding rather than as primary British-literature answers.

sections.name: Old English & Anglo-Saxon Literature
sections.blurb: Alliterative verse and prose in the earliest form of English, running from Caedmon through the Norman Conquest of 1066.

entries.note: Anonymous Old English epic of a Geatish hero who slays Grendel, Grendel's mother, and a dragon.
Earliest stage of the English language, used in Beowulf and Anglo-Saxon poetry before roughly 1150.
Monster slain by the hero in Beowulf, along with Grendel's mother and later a dragon.
Old English poetic form preserved in the Exeter Book, posing clever double-meaning puzzles.
Key structural device of Old English verse, linking stressed syllables across the poetic line as in Beowulf.
Earliest known named English poet, a herdsman said to have received the gift of song in a dream.
Old English dream-vision poem in which the cross (rood) itself narrates Christ's crucifixion.
Anglo-Saxon monk-historian; the Ecclesiastical History of the English People, "the father of English history."
Major manuscript anthology of Old English poetry, preserving riddles, elegies, and religious verse.
Vengeful monster slain by Beowulf after he kills her son Grendel.
Compound metaphorical phrases, such as "whale-road" for the sea, characteristic of Old English poetry.
Legendary early medieval Welsh bard credited with a body of Brythonic praise poetry.
Anglo-Saxon monk-historian; the Ecclesiastical History of the English People, "the father of English history."

sections.name: Middle English Literature
sections.blurb: Vernacular poetry and prose written after 1066 under Norman and continental influence, culminating in Chaucer's generation.

entries.note: Father of English poetry; The Canterbury Tales and Troilus and Criseyde, written in Middle English.
Chaucer's frame narrative of pilgrims to Canterbury telling tales, including the Wife of Bath and the Pardoner.
Stage of the English language, used by Chaucer and the Gawain-poet, between Old English and the modern tongue.

entries.topic: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
entries.answerline: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
entries.note: Anonymous alliterative romance in which Gawain accepts a beheading challenge from a mysterious green knight.
Anonymous medieval morality play in which the allegorical figure Everyman is summoned by Death.
Chaucer's oft-married, outspoken pilgrim in The Canterbury Tales who argues for female authority in marriage.
Anonymous author credited with Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, Pearl, and other alliterative Middle English poems.
Middle English poet and contemporary of Chaucer; Confessio Amantis, a collection of moral tales.
Medieval allegorical drama genre, exemplified by Everyman, teaching moral lessons through personified virtues and vices.
Arthurian knight who accepts the Green Knight's beheading challenge in the alliterative romance bearing his name.
Early Tudor humanist statesman and author of Utopia, executed under Henry VIII.
First and longest tale in Chaucer's Canterbury Tales, of two knights' rivalry for the same woman.
Corrupt seller of indulgences among Chaucer's Canterbury Tales pilgrims, whose tale warns against greed.
Thomas More's work describing an ideal imagined island society, coining the term "utopia."
Introduced the printing press to England in 1476, printing Chaucer's Canterbury Tales and Malory's Morte d'Arthur.
Bawdy fabliau from Chaucer's Canterbury Tales involving a cuckolded carpenter and a misdirected kiss.
Arthurian knight who accepts the Green Knight's beheading challenge in the alliterative romance bearing his name.
Early Tudor poet known for "Skeltonics," short, driving rhymed lines bridging medieval and Renaissance verse.
Medieval English mystic and anchoress; Revelations of Divine Love, the earliest surviving English-language book by a woman.
Legendary British king whose court and knights, including Gawain, populate medieval English romance.
Term for medieval and early-modern Scottish court poets, including Dunbar and Henryson.
Anglo-Norman poet at the English court who wrote the Lais, short verse romances.
Anonymous elegiac dream-vision poem, attributed to the Gawain-poet, mourning a lost child or "pearl."
William Langland's allegorical dream-vision poem following Piers the Plowman's search for religious truth.
Early Tudor humanist statesman and author of Utopia, executed under Henry VIII.
Trojan prince whose doomed love for Criseyde is chronicled in Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde.
Chaucer's tragic romance of a Trojan prince betrayed by his beloved Criseyde for the Greek Diomedes.
Chaucer's Canterbury tale of three revelers who find death by greedily fighting over gold.
Thomas Malory's compilation of Arthurian legend, printed by Caxton in 1485.
Medieval biblical drama cycles performed by town guilds, precursors to English secular theater.
Legendary English outlaw of medieval ballads, robbing the rich to aid the poor from Sherwood Forest.
Chaucer's early dream-vision elegy, likely written for John of Gaunt's deceased first wife.

entries.topic: the General Prologue to the Canterbury Tales
entries.answerline: the General Prologue to the Canterbury Tales
entries.note: Chaucer's opening frame introducing each of the pilgrims journeying to Canterbury.
Drunken, bawdy pilgrim among Chaucer's Canterbury Tales narrators, teller of "The Miller's Tale."
Northern English town giving its name to the Wakefield Cycle of medieval mystery plays.

sections.name: Elizabethan & Jacobean Literature
sections.blurb: Poetry and drama of Shakespeare's era and the reigns of Elizabeth I and James I, from sonnet sequences to revenge tragedy.

entries.note: Jacobean playwright and poet, Shakespeare's rival and friend; Volpone, The Alchemist, Every Man in His Humour.
Elizabethan dramatist and Shakespeare's rival; Doctor Faustus, Tamburlaine the Great, The Jew of Malta.
Elizabethan-Jacobean playwright and poet; tragedies, comedies, and histories plus 154 sonnets; central figure of English drama.
Shakespeare tragedy of an aging king who divides his kingdom among his daughters and descends into madness.
Elizabethan poet of the allegorical epic The Faerie Queene and the sonnet sequence Amoretti.
Spenser's unfinished allegorical epic honoring Elizabeth I through knights personifying virtues, including the Redcrosse Knight.
Shakespeare's tragedy of the Danish prince who feigns madness to avenge his father, murdered by Claudius.
Shakespeare comedy of sparring lovers Beatrice and Benedick alongside the slandered Hero and Claudio.
Elizabethan courtier-poet; sonnet sequence Astrophil and Stella, prose romance The Countess of Pembroke's Arcadia.
Shakespeare's late romance of exiled magician Prospero, his daughter Miranda, and spirits Ariel and Caliban.
Shakespeare pastoral comedy set in the Forest of Arden, featuring the cross-dressing Rosalind and melancholy Jaques.
Shakespeare tragedy of a Scottish general driven by prophecy and Lady Macbeth's ambition to murder King Duncan.
Shakespeare history play of the scheming, physically deformed Richard's ruthless rise to the English throne.
Jacobean revenge tragedian; The Duchess of Malfi, on a widowed duchess destroyed by her vengeful brothers.
Shakespeare tragedy of the Moorish general destroyed by Iago's manipulation into jealousy over Desdemona.
Shakespeare's earliest, bloodiest tragedy, featuring the Roman general's revenge against Tamora and Aaron.
Ben Jonson's satirical comedy of a Venetian miser who feigns death to swindle legacy-hunters.
Shakespeare history play following the young king's rousing leadership at the Battle of Agincourt.
Shakespeare "problem play" of a deputy's abuse of power and a novice nun's moral test.
Ben Jonson's comedy of con artists running elaborate scams from a house in plague-emptied London.
Shakespeare comedy of a king and his lords who forswear women's company, only to promptly fall in love.
Shakespeare's comic, cowardly knight and companion to Prince Hal in the Henry IV plays.
Shakespeare comedy in which moneylender Shylock demands a pound of flesh from merchant Antonio.
Shakespeare comedy of Petruchio's contentious courtship and "taming" of the sharp-tongued Katherina.
Shakespeare comedy of shipwrecked twin Viola disguised as a man, entangling Duke Orsino and Countess Olivia.
Enslaved, resentful native of the island in Shakespeare's The Tempest, son of the witch Sycorax.
Shakespeare tragedy of a proud Roman general whose contempt for the plebeians leads to his banishment.
Marlowe's tragedy of a scholar who trades his soul to Mephistophilis for twenty-four years of magical power.
Marlowe's tragedy of vengeful Maltese merchant Barabas, a forerunner to Shakespeare's Shylock.
Egyptian queen and Mark Antony's lover in Shakespeare's Antony and Cleopatra.
Scheming ensign in Shakespeare's Othello whose manipulations drive the Moor to murder his wife.
Roman triumvir whose love for Cleopatra destroys his political standing in Shakespeare's tragedy.
Elizabethan courtier-poet, author of Astrophil and Stella; usually styled Sir Philip Sidney.
John Webster's revenge tragedy of a widowed duchess destroyed by her tyrannical brothers for remarrying beneath her rank.

entries.topic: Twelfth Night, or What You Will
entries.answerline: Twelfth Night, or What You Will
entries.note: Full title of Shakespeare's comedy of shipwrecked twin Viola disguised as a man.
Tudor queen whose reign gave its name to Elizabethan literature and who was celebrated in Spenser's Faerie Queene.
Elizabethan-Jacobean poet and playwright best known for his translation of Homer.
Puritanical, self-important steward tricked into absurd behavior in Shakespeare's Twelfth Night.
Romeo's witty, ill-fated friend in Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet, killed by Tybalt.
Hamlet's beloved, driven to madness and drowning after her father Polonius's death in Shakespeare's tragedy.
Clever heroine of The Merchant of Venice who disguises herself as a lawyer to save Antonio.
Shakespeare's tragedy of two feuding Verona families' children whose secret marriage ends in double suicide.
Mysterious, dark-haired woman addressed in a group of Shakespeare's sonnets.
Anonymous (often attributed to Thomas Middleton) Jacobean revenge tragedy featuring the avenger Vindice.
Shakespeare's early comedy of friendship and romantic rivalry between Valentine and Proteus.
Prolific Jacobean playwright; The Changeling, A Chaste Maid in Cheapside, likely co-author of The Revenger's Tragedy.
Early Tudor courtier-poet who introduced the sonnet form to English poetry.
Christopher Marlowe's pastoral lyric inviting a beloved to "come live with me and be my love."
Sir Philip Sidney's pioneering English sonnet sequence chronicling a courtier's unrequited love.
King Lear's youngest, most honest daughter, disowned for refusing to flatter him, later reconciled before his death.
Othello's devoted wife, falsely suspected of infidelity and murdered by him in Shakespeare's tragedy.
Jacobean-Caroline playwright; A New Way to Pay Old Debts, known for the villain Sir Giles Overreach.
Mischievous fairy also called Robin Goodfellow who causes the romantic mix-ups in A Midsummer Night's Dream.
Middleton and Rowley's Jacobean tragedy of Beatrice-Joanna's murderous scheme to avoid an unwanted marriage.
154-poem sequence including the Fair Youth and Dark Lady sequences and "Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?"
Shakespeare comedy of lovers, fairies, and the mischievous Puck tangled in an enchanted forest.
Edmund Spenser's sonnet sequence celebrating his courtship of his second wife, Elizabeth Boyle.
Ben Jonson's sprawling comedy satirizing Puritan hypocrisy and London fairground life.
Wedding-poem genre, notably Spenser's own "Epithalamion" celebrating his marriage.
Ben Jonson's comedy satirizing character types defined by their dominant "humour," or temperament.
1623 posthumous collection of Shakespeare's plays, the first to preserve many of them in print.
Shakespeare's history trilogy on the weak king's reign amid the Wars of the Roses.
Marlowe's unfinished mythological poem of two lovers separated by the Hellespont, completed by Chapman.
Jacobean satirist and playwright known for The Malcontent and biting verse satires.
Historical duke whose "this England" deathbed speech appears in Shakespeare's Richard II.
Shakespeare tragedy of the Roman dictator's assassination by conspirators led by Brutus and Cassius.
Ambitious wife who goads Macbeth into regicide, later undone by guilt-driven sleepwalking and madness.
Scottish nobleman "not of woman born" who avenges his murdered family by killing Macbeth.
Knight of Holiness who battles the dragon in Book One of Spenser's The Faerie Queene.
Early Tudor courtier-poet who introduced the sonnet form to English poetry.
154-poem sequence including the Fair Youth and Dark Lady sequences.
Beloved addressed in Sir Philip Sidney's sonnet sequence Astrophil and Stella.
Marlowe's play glorifying the ruthless conqueror's rise from shepherd to world-conquering emperor.
Shakespeare's farce of two sets of separated identical twins causing chaotic mistaken identity.

entries.topic: The Spanish Tragedy, or Hieronimo Is Mad Again
entries.answerline: The Spanish Tragedy, or Hieronimo Is Mad Again
entries.note: Thomas Kyd's foundational revenge tragedy, whose grieving father Hieronimo seeks vengeance for his murdered son.
Late Shakespeare-Fletcher collaboration of two cousins whose friendship is destroyed by rivalry over the same woman.
Jacobean playwright; The Shoemaker's Holiday, a comedy celebrating London tradesmen.
Shakespeare tragedy of a generous Athenian ruined by his fair-weather friends who becomes a bitter misanthrope.
Witty court fool who accompanies Rosalind and Celia into exile in Shakespeare's As You Like It.
Title figure of Mary Wroth's Jacobean prose romance The Countess of Montgomery's Urania.
Italian city setting of Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet and The Two Gentlemen of Verona.
Dead court jester whose skull Hamlet contemplates, mourning "Alas, poor Yorick."

entries.topic: "Shall I compare thee to a Summer's day?"
entries.answerline: "Shall I compare thee to a Summer's day?"
entries.note: Opening line of Shakespeare's Sonnet 18, praising a beloved's beauty as more lasting than a summer's day.
John Ford's Caroline tragedy of an incestuous love between a brother and sister that ends in violent ruin.
Thomas Middleton's Jacobean city comedy satirizing sexual and financial scheming among London tradesfolk.

entries.topic: A New Way to Pay Old Debts
entries.answerline: A New Way to Pay Old Debts
entries.note: Philip Massinger's Jacobean-Caroline comedy featuring the grasping villain Sir Giles Overreach.
Shakespeare "problem play" in which Helena cures the king and tricks her reluctant husband Bertram into love.
Shakespeare tragedy of the Roman triumvir's ruinous love for the Egyptian queen.
Vengeful Maltese merchant-villain of Marlowe's The Jew of Malta.
Sharp-tongued heroine of Much Ado About Nothing, who spars wittily with Benedick before falling for him.
Hamlet's uncle, who murders his brother the king, marries the widowed queen, and is finally killed by Hamlet.
Shakespeare's late romance combining a wronged queen's daughter, disguise, and reconciliation among warring Britain and Rome.
Mark Antony's loyal follower in Antony and Cleopatra, who deserts him and dies of grief and shame.
Supernatural beings, led by Oberon and Titania, who meddle in the lovers' affairs in A Midsummer Night's Dream.
Wise-seeming comic figures such as King Lear's Fool and Twelfth Night's Feste, licensed to speak uncomfortable truths.
Pastoral woodland refuge for exiled characters in Shakespeare's As You Like It.
Shakespeare (and Fletcher) history play on the Tudor king's reign, also the historical monarch served by Thomas Cromwell.
Jacobean playwright who succeeded Shakespeare as the King's Men's chief dramatist, often his collaborator.
Young heroine of Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet, who dies alongside her secret husband.
Wise-seeming jester who accompanies and mocks Lear during his descent into madness.
Demon who serves and ultimately claims the soul of the title scholar in Marlowe's Doctor Faustus.
Elizabethan poet; Poly-Olbion, a vast topographical poem celebrating England's landscape.
Prospero's innocent daughter in The Tempest, who marvels at humanity in a "brave new world."
Shakespeare's brooding Danish prince who feigns madness to avenge his murdered father.
Exiled magician-duke who commands the spirit Ariel and rules the island in Shakespeare's The Tempest.
Fairy who delivers dreams according to Mercutio's speech in Romeo and Juliet; also namesake of an early Shelley poem.
Nicholas Udall's mid-16th-century play, often called the first English comedy.
Shakespeare history play on the deposition of an ineffectual king by Bolingbroke, later Henry IV.
Hamlet's interchangeable courtier acquaintances, sent to their deaths carrying a forged letter.
Jewish moneylender in The Merchant of Venice who demands a pound of flesh from Antonio.
Elizabethan courtier, explorer, and poet, executed under James I.
Fourteen-line poetic form introduced to England by Wyatt and perfected by Shakespeare.
Genre of linked sonnets exploring a single love story, pioneered in English by Sidney's Astrophil and Stella.
Sir Philip Sidney's prose pastoral romance, dedicated to his sister.

entries.topic: The Life and Death of King John
entries.answerline: The Life and Death of King John
entries.note: Shakespeare history play dramatizing the troubled reign of the king forced to sign the Magna Carta.
Edmund Spenser's early pastoral poem cycle organized into twelve months, launching his poetic career.
Thomas Kyd's foundational revenge tragedy, whose grieving father Hieronimo seeks vengeance for his murdered son.
John Webster's Jacobean revenge tragedy of the adulteress Vittoria Corombona's downfall and murder.
Shakespeare's late romance of a jealous king's wrongful accusation and the eventual, seemingly miraculous reunion of his family.
Dramatic mode of downfall and suffering perfected by Shakespeare and the Jacobean revenge dramatists.

sections.name: Metaphysical & Civil War-Era Poetry
sections.blurb: Seventeenth-century poets known for wit and conceits, alongside Milton and the literature surrounding the English Civil War.

entries.note: Founder of Metaphysical poetry; Holy Sonnets and elaborate conceits; later Dean of St. Paul's writing devotional prose.
Puritan poet of the English Civil War era; epic Paradise Lost, Areopagitica, Samson Agonistes, "Lycidas."
Metaphysical poet and Civil War-era politician; "To His Coy Mistress," "The Garden."
Metaphysical poet-priest; The Temple, devotional lyrics including shaped poems like "Easter Wings."
Cavalier poet loyal to Charles I; "To Althea, from Prison," "To Lucasta, Going to the Wars."
John Donne's devotional sonnet sequence, including "Death, be not proud" and "Batter my heart."
Milton's epic on Satan's fall and Adam and Eve's expulsion from Eden, justifying "the ways of God to men."
Puritan Lord Protector of England after the Civil War execution of Charles I; subject of Marvell's Horatian Ode.
Cavalier poet; "To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time," carpe-diem lyrics of nature and love.
Milton's pastoral elegy mourning a drowned Cambridge classmate under the shepherd name Lycidas.
Rebellious fallen angel and antihero of Milton's Paradise Lost, who declares it "better to reign in Hell."
Andrew Marvell's carpe-diem poem urging a reluctant lover on given "Time's winged chariot."
John Donne's farewell poem comparing separated lovers to the two legs of a drafting compass.
Milton's closet drama on the blinded, captive biblical strongman's final act of vengeance against the Philistines.
17th-century physician and prose stylist; Religio Medici, meditative essays on faith and mortality.
Opening line of a John Donne Holy Sonnet begging violent divine intervention to reform the speaker's soul.
Milton's companion poem to "L'Allegro," praising the contemplative, melancholic life.
Donne's poem defiantly elevating romantic love to the status of religious sainthood.
17th-century Metaphysical-adjacent poet known for Pindaric odes and elaborate conceits.
Milton's prose polemic against licensing and for freedom of the press.
Group of 17th-century royalist poets, including Lovelace and Herrick, prizing wit and carpe-diem themes.
17th-century group including Donne, Herbert, and Marvell, known for intellectual conceits and paradox.
John Donne's aubade celebrating awakened, all-consuming love between two united souls.
Richard Lovelace's Cavalier poem declaring that "stone walls do not a prison make."
First man, expelled from Eden alongside Eve in Milton's Paradise Lost.
Izaak Walton's mid-17th-century prose treatise celebrating the art and contemplative pleasure of fishing.

entries.topic: "I am a little world made cunningly"
entries.answerline: "I am a little world made cunningly"
entries.note: John Donne Holy Sonnet comparing the speaker's soul to a microcosm requiring purification.
Dawn-song poetic form used by Donne and later revisited by Larkin.
Milton's masque in which a virtuous Lady resists the temptations of the seductive sorcerer Comus.
First woman, tempted by Satan and expelled from Eden alongside Adam in Milton's Paradise Lost.
Cavalier poet known for witty, casual love lyrics such as "Why so pale and wan, fond lover?"
Beloved addressed in Richard Lovelace's Cavalier poems, including "To Lucasta, Going to the Wars."
Elaborate court entertainment genre combining music, dance, and allegory, exemplified by Milton's Comus.
17th-century poetic style marked by intellectual conceits and paradox, led by Donne, Herbert, and Marvell.
Religious reformers whose Civil War-era ascendancy shaped Milton's writing and closed the theaters.
Cavalier poet known for witty, casual love lyrics such as "Why so pale and wan, fond lover?"
17th-century physician and prose stylist; Religio Medici, meditative essays on faith and mortality.
George Herbert's posthumously published collection of devotional lyrics, including shaped poems like "Easter Wings."

sections.name: Restoration & Augustan Literature
sections.blurb: Comedy of manners, satire, and the rise of the novel from the 1660 Restoration through the mid-eighteenth century.

entries.note: First Englishwoman to earn a living by writing; Restoration playwright and novelist of Oroonoko and The Rover.
Restoration Poet Laureate and satirist; Mac Flecknoe, Absalom and Achitophel, All for Love.
Augustan novelist and magistrate; picaresque Tom Jones and Joseph Andrews.
Anglo-Irish satirist and Dean of St. Patrick's; Gulliver's Travels, "A Modest Proposal," A Tale of a Tub.
Augustan satirist and translator of Homer; The Rape of the Lock, The Dunciad, An Essay on Man.
Augustan lexicographer and critic; compiled A Dictionary of the English Language; subject of Boswell's Life.
Anglo-Irish playwright of comedy of manners in the Restoration style; The Rivals, The School for Scandal, The Critic.
Oliver Goldsmith's comedy of mistaken identity in which a suitor is tricked into thinking a manor house is an inn.
Augustan-era journalist and novelist; Robinson Crusoe and Moll Flanders, early landmarks of the English novel.
Anglo-Irish Augustan writer; She Stoops to Conquer, The Vicar of Wakefield, "The Deserted Village."
Restoration comedy-of-manners playwright; The Way of the World, prized for epigrammatic wit.
18th-century novelist; Tristram Shandy, a digressive, typographically experimental mock-autobiography.
Laurence Sterne's digressive mock-autobiography narrated by a man who struggles even to be born within the story.

entries.topic: The Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy, Gentleman
entries.answerline: The Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy, Gentleman
entries.note: Full title of Sterne's digressive 18th-century mock-autobiography.
Daniel Defoe's novel of a shipwrecked castaway who survives alone on an island before meeting his companion Friday.
Richard Brinsley Sheridan's comedy of manners skewering hypocrisy and gossip among London society.
Augustan-era epistolary novelist; Pamela and Clarissa, pioneering the novel of interior feeling.
William Wycherley's bawdy Restoration comedy involving the seducer Horner's feigned impotence.
Comic character in Sheridan's The Rivals whose habitual word confusions gave rise to the term "malapropism."
18th-century picaresque novelist; Roderick Random, The Expedition of Humphry Clinker.
Early 18th-century novelist and playwright, prolific in the amatory fiction genre before the realist novel.
Augustan-era biographer; The Life of Samuel Johnson, a landmark of the biographical form.
Nonconformist preacher and author of the allegory The Pilgrim's Progress, following Christian's journey to salvation.
Alexander Pope's mock-epic satire crowning a "king of dunces" to mock literary hacks.
Sheridan's comedy of manners featuring the malaprop-prone Mrs. Malaprop and rival suitors for Lydia Languish.
Aphra Behn's Restoration comedy of exiled English cavaliers pursuing romance in Naples during carnival.
William Congreve's comedy of manners centered on the witty courtship of Mirabell and Millamant.
Jonathan Swift's satirical essay proposing the Irish poor sell their children as food for the wealthy.
Jonathan Swift's satirical prose allegory mocking religious and scholarly excess.
18th-century actor-manager who reinvigorated Shakespearean performance and Georgian theatre.
Surname of Augustan novelist Henry Fielding, author of Tom Jones and Joseph Andrews.
Alexander Pope's mock-heroic poem elevating a stolen lock of hair to epic proportions.
Henry Fielding's picaresque novel following a foundling's misadventures to eventual legitimacy and marriage.
Alexander Pope's philosophical poem defending providence, arguing "Whatever IS, is RIGHT."
Everyman pilgrim-hero of John Bunyan's allegory The Pilgrim's Progress, journeying to the Celestial City.
Period beginning with Charles II's 1660 return to the throne, marked by comedy of manners and Dryden's poetry.
Companion rescued from cannibals by the title character of Defoe's Robinson Crusoe.
Land of tiny people visited by the narrator in the first voyage of Swift's Gulliver's Travels.
Daniel Defoe's novel narrated by a woman who survives through theft, marriage, and reinvention.
Restoration-era diarist whose secret shorthand journal vividly records the Great Fire and Plague of London.

entries.topic: The History of Tom Jones, a Foundling
entries.answerline: The History of Tom Jones, a Foundling
entries.note: Full title of Henry Fielding's picaresque novel of a foundling's misadventures to legitimacy.
Oliver Goldsmith's sentimental novel of a clergyman's family enduring reversals of fortune.
Restoration playwright and poet laureate satirized as the dull heir to bad poetry in Dryden's Mac Flecknoe.
Alexander Pope's satirical verse letter defending himself against literary enemies, including the portrait of "Sporus."
Oliver Goldsmith's poem lamenting rural depopulation and the loss of a idealized English village.
Monarch whose 1660 restoration to the throne ended the Interregnum and reopened the theaters.
Samuel Richardson's epistolary novel of a woman's ruin and death after being drugged and violated by Lovelace.
18th-century novelist and diarist; Evelina, an early novel of a young woman's entrance into society.
Historic London street synonymous with impoverished hack writers and journalists in the 18th century.
Narrator-surgeon of Swift's Gulliver's Travels, shipwrecked among Lilliputians, Brobdingnagians, and Houyhnhnms.
Period beginning with Charles II's 1660 return to the throne, marked by comedy of manners and satire.
Anglo-Irish playwright of comedy of manners; The Rivals, The School for Scandal.
John Bunyan's allegory following Christian's journey from the City of Destruction to the Celestial City.
Restoration comedy-of-manners playwright; The Country Wife, known for bawdy wit.

entries.topic: Clarissa, or, the History of a Young Lady
entries.answerline: Clarissa, or, the History of a Young Lady
entries.note: Full title of Samuel Richardson's epistolary novel of a woman's ruin at the hands of Lovelace.
Actor-playwright and poet laureate mocked as a symbol of bad taste, later crowned "king of dunces" in Pope's Dunciad.
Augustan lexicographer and critic; compiled A Dictionary of the English Language; subject of Boswell's Life.
Frances Burney's epistolary novel of a young woman's awkward, comic entrance into fashionable society.
18th-century novelist and diarist; Evelina, an early novel of a young woman's entrance into society.
Samuel Butler's mock-heroic poem satirizing Puritan hypocrisy through a bungling knight-errant.
Augustan satirist and physician; member of the Scriblerus Club alongside Pope and Swift.
Henry Fielding's picaresque novel parodying Richardson's Pamela through a virtuous footman.
John Dryden's mock-heroic satire crowning a bad poet, Thomas Shadwell, as heir to a kingdom of dullness.
Samuel Richardson's epistolary novel of a servant girl who preserves her virtue and is ultimately rewarded with marriage.
Full title of Samuel Richardson's epistolary novel of a virtuous servant girl.
Sentimental clergyman-narrator of Sterne's A Sentimental Journey, also a character in Tristram Shandy.
18th-century novelist and sister of Henry Fielding; The Adventures of David Simple.
Mode of mocking critique that dominates 18th-century English writing, from Swift to Pope.
Beautiful, virtuous love interest of the title character in Fielding's Tom Jones.
Margaret Cavendish's proto-science-fiction romance of a woman who becomes empress of a fantastical parallel world.
Richard Brinsley Sheridan's satirical play mocking theatrical convention through a rehearsal-within-a-play.

entries.topic: The Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy
entries.answerline: The Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy
entries.note: Laurence Sterne's digressive mock-autobiography, narrated by a man who struggles even to be born within the story.
Henry Mackenzie's sentimental novel following the excessively tender-hearted Harley through a series of tearful encounters.
George Etherege's Restoration comedy of manners centered on the rake Dorimant.
Period beginning with Charles II's 1660 return to the throne, marked by comedy of manners and Dryden's poetry.
Full name of 18th-century picaresque novelist Tobias Smollett.

sections.name: Romanticism
sections.blurb: Poets and novelists of imagination, nature, and revolutionary feeling, writing from the 1780s through the 1830s.

entries.note: Second-generation Romantic poet; odes including "Ode on a Grecian Urn" and "To Autumn"; died young of tuberculosis.
Visionary Romantic poet-engraver; Songs of Innocence and of Experience, "The Tyger," prophetic books like Jerusalem.
Radical Romantic poet; "Ozymandias," "Ode to the West Wind," Prometheus Unbound; married Mary Shelley.
Romantic poet and critic; "The Rime of the Ancient Mariner," "Kubla Khan"; collaborated with Wordsworth on Lyrical Ballads.
Lake Poet who launched Romanticism with Coleridge; The Prelude, "Tintern Abbey," "I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud."
Romantic poet of scandalous fame; Don Juan, Childe Harold's Pilgrimage; died aiding Greek independence.
Novelist of manners in the Regency era; Pride and Prejudice, Emma, Sense and Sensibility, Persuasion.
Scotland's national poet, writing largely in Scots dialect; "Auld Lang Syne," "Tam o' Shanter."
Scottish Romantic novelist who invented the historical novel; Ivanhoe, Rob Roy, Waverley.
Graveyard-school poet and precursor to Romanticism; "Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard."
Jane Austen novel of the meddling matchmaker Emma Woodhouse, who misreads the romantic lives of Highbury's residents.
Romantic-era essayist known for Confessions of an English Opium-Eater.
Byron's unfinished mock-epic satirizing European society through its rakish (if passive) hero's adventures.
Jane Austen novel of quiet, principled Fanny Price raised among her wealthy Bertram relations.
Romantic-era essayist and critic; Characters of Shakespeare's Plays, known for vigorous prose style.
Thomas Gray's meditation on mortality and obscure rural lives, source of "the paths of glory lead but to the grave."
Mary Shelley's novel of scientist Victor Frankenstein and the nameless creature he assembles and abandons.
Jane Austen's novel parodying Gothic fiction through impressionable heroine Catherine Morland.
Jane Austen's debut novel contrasting practical Elinor and passionate Marianne Dashwood.
Jane Austen's novel of Elizabeth Bennet's evolving relationship with the proud Mr. Darcy.
Lake Poet and Poet Laureate alongside Wordsworth and Coleridge; wrote "The Story of the Three Bears."
Coleridge's opium-inspired, unfinished vision of Xanadu and its "stately pleasure-dome."
Romantic-era essayist; Essays of Elia, and co-author with sister Mary of Tales from Shakespeare.
Meddling, well-meaning heroine of Jane Austen's Emma who misjudges the romances around her.
Full title of Mary Shelley's novel of scientist Victor Frankenstein and his abandoned creature.
Full name and title of Romantic poet Lord Byron.
Romantic-era novelist; Frankenstein, written partly in a ghost-story contest with Byron and Percy Shelley.
Jane Austen's final completed novel, reuniting Anne Elliot with her former suitor Captain Wentworth.

entries.topic: "Ode: Intimations of Immortality from Recollections of Early Childhood"
entries.answerline: "Ode: Intimations of Immortality from Recollections of Early Childhood"
entries.note: Wordsworth's ode on childhood's visionary "clouds of glory" fading into ordinary adult perception.
Percy Bysshe Shelley's ode addressing the bird as a "blithe spirit" of unearthly joyful song.
John Keats's ode celebrating the "season of mists and mellow fruitfulness."
Pioneering Gothic novelist; The Mysteries of Udolpho, known for explained supernatural terror.
Walter Scott's historical novel of Saxon knight Wilfred of Ivanhoe amid Norman-Saxon tension under Richard the Lionheart.
Matthew Lewis's lurid Gothic novel of a monk's corruption through lust and a demonic pact.
Romantic-era satirical novelist; Nightmare Abbey, poking fun at Gothic excess and his poet friends.
Scottish Romantic novelist who invented the historical novel; Ivanhoe, Rob Roy, Waverley.
Coleridge's conversation poem addressing his infant son while watching frost form outside at night.
Keats's ode meditating on a frozen artwork, concluding "Beauty is truth, truth beauty."
Keats's ode contrasting the bird's timeless song with human suffering and mortality.
Shelley's ode invoking the wind as a force of revolutionary renewal, asking "if Winter comes, can Spring be far behind?"
Robert Burns's Scots-dialect narrative poem of a drunk farmer chased by witches after spying their revel.
Coleridge's narrative poem of a sailor's supernatural punishment for killing an albatross.

variants.answerline: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner

entries.note: City invoked in William Blake's poem envisioning a New Jerusalem built in "England's green and pleasant Land."
Anglo-Irish novelist; Castle Rackrent, an early regional and historical novel narrated by a family servant.
Thomas Love Peacock's satirical novel mocking Gothic fiction and his Romantic poet contemporaries.
Series title under which Walter Scott published several of his Waverley novels.
Horace Walpole's 1764 novel, generally credited as the first Gothic novel.
Ann Radcliffe's Gothic novel of orphaned Emily St. Aubert's terrors in a sinister Italian castle.
Wordsworth's autobiographical epic on "the growth of a poet's mind," published posthumously.
Pre-Romantic poet and hymn writer; The Task, known for gentle, personal verse on nature and faith.
Coleridge's unfinished narrative poem of a young woman entranced by the sinister, possibly supernatural Geraldine.
Keats's ode arguing that true melancholy is inseparable from beauty and joy's fleeting nature.
Keats's ode addressing the neglected goddess Psyche and vowing to become her priest.
Keats's narrative poem of lovers Madeline and Porphyro uniting on a night of medieval superstition.
Wordsworth's poem on a Highland girl's haunting song, heard but not understood by the passing poet.
William Blake's Songs of Experience poem asking what immortal hand or eye could frame the tiger's fearsome symmetry.
Term for Coleridge's intimate, meditative blank-verse poems addressed to a silent listener, such as "Frost at Midnight."
Quiet, morally steadfast heroine of Jane Austen's Mansfield Park.
Nameless, articulate creature assembled and abandoned by the scientist in Mary Shelley's Frankenstein.
Emma Woodhouse's naive protegee in Austen's Emma, whose romantic prospects Emma repeatedly misjudges.
Author of The Castle of Otranto, generally credited as the first Gothic novel.
Scottish Romantic-era writer; The Private Memoirs and Confessions of a Justified Sinner.
Romantic-era "peasant poet" celebrated for close observation of the English countryside.
Romantic-era poet and essayist, friend and champion of Keats and Shelley.
Wordsworth's short elegiac poem sequence mourning an unidentified young woman named Lucy.
Percy Bysshe Shelley's lyrical drama reimagining the Titan's liberation as a myth of revolutionary hope.
Walter Scott's historical novel romanticizing the Scottish outlaw Rob Roy MacGregor.
Unwelcome visitor blamed by Coleridge for interrupting his composition of "Kubla Khan."
Percy Bysshe Shelley's long allegorical poem of revolution and idealistic reform.
Pre-Romantic "marvellous boy" poet who forged medieval verse and died by suicide at seventeen.
William Beckford's Gothic Orientalist novel of a caliph's descent into damnation through forbidden knowledge.
Surname of Lake Poet William Wordsworth, who launched Romanticism with Coleridge.
Percy Bysshe Shelley's essay declaring poets "the unacknowledged legislators of the world."
Robert Burns's Scots-dialect poem of remembrance, traditionally sung to mark the New Year.
John Clare's poem of self-erasure and isolation, written during his confinement in an asylum.
Wordsworth's poem recalling a field of daffodils that later returns to comfort him in solitude.
Keats's ballad of a knight seduced and abandoned by a supernatural, merciless lady.
Shelley's sonnet on a shattered desert statue of a once-mighty king, mocking the futility of tyrannical pride.
Wordsworth's poem in which an old leech-gatherer's stoic endurance steadies the poet's despair.
Thomas De Quincey's essay evoking the speed and sublime terror of nighttime mail-coach travel.

entries.topic: "The World is Too Much With Us"
entries.answerline: "The World is Too Much With Us"
entries.note: Wordsworth's sonnet lamenting that "getting and spending" has estranged humanity from nature.
Wordsworth's poem reflecting on memory and nature while revisiting a ruined abbey on the River Wye.
Small creature addressed in Robert Burns's poem "To a Mouse," source of "the best-laid schemes."
Austen's Persuasion heroine, reunited with her former suitor Captain Wentworth after years of regret.
Maria Edgeworth's novel narrated by a family servant, an early regional and historical novel.
Impressionable heroine of Austen's Northanger Abbey, whose Gothic-novel fantasies are gently mocked.
Byron's narrative poem following a disillusioned wanderer through Europe, popularizing the "Byronic hero."
Alias meaning "the Disinherited," used by the title character while incognito in Scott's Ivanhoe.
Poet and diarist, sister of William Wordsworth, whose journals influenced and recorded his poetry.
Keats's long mythological poem opening "A thing of beauty is a joy for ever," on a shepherd loved by the moon goddess.
Full name of popular Romantic-era poet Felicia Hemans, known for "Casabianca" ("The boy stood on the burning deck").
Popular Romantic-era poet known for "Casabianca" ("The boy stood on the burning deck").
Minor Romantic-era poet, elder son of Samuel Taylor Coleridge.
Fourteen-line poems by the Romantic poet, including "On First Looking into Chapman's Homer."
Byron's physician, author of "The Vampyre," an early and influential English vampire tale.
Wordsworth and Coleridge's 1798 collection, generally regarded as launching English Romanticism.
Passionate, impulsive heroine of Austen's Sense and Sensibility, contrasted with practical sister Elinor.
Keats's concept, from his letters, of the capacity to remain in uncertainty without irritable reaching after fact.
Jane Austen's unfinished final novel, satirizing a seaside resort's speculative development.
Fourteen-line poems by the Romantic poet, including "On First Looking into Chapman's Homer."
Full name of Romantic-era essayist Thomas De Quincey, author of Confessions of an English Opium-Eater.
Romantic-era poet and prose writer; Imaginary Conversations, a series of dialogues between historical figures.
Full title of Walter Scott's novel that launched the historical-novel genre and his Waverley series.

sections.name: Victorian Literature
sections.blurb: The novel's golden age and the poetry of an industrializing empire, spanning Dickens and the Brontes to the fin de siecle.

entries.note: Victorian novelist and poet of Wessex; Tess of the d'Urbervilles, Jude the Obscure, and Far from the Madding Crowd.
Victorian Jesuit poet who invented sprung rhythm; "The Windhover," "Pied Beauty," "The Wreck of the Deutschland."
Victorian novelist of serialized social fiction; Great Expectations, Bleak House, Oliver Twist, A Tale of Two Cities.
Irish wit of Aestheticism and Decadence; The Picture of Dorian Gray, The Importance of Being Earnest; imprisoned for "gross indecency."
Victorian Poet Laureate; In Memoriam A.H.H., "The Charge of the Light Brigade," "The Lady of Shalott."
Pen name of Mary Ann Evans, Victorian realist novelist; Middlemarch, The Mill on the Floss, Silas Marner.
Charlotte Bronte's novel of an orphaned governess who marries the Byronic Mr. Rochester despite his hidden mad wife.
Victorian master of the dramatic monologue; "My Last Duchess," "Porphyria's Lover"; married Elizabeth Barrett Browning.
Victorian poet-critic; "Dover Beach," "The Scholar-Gipsy"; championed "culture" in Culture and Anarchy.
Anglo-Indian writer of empire; The Jungle Book, Kim, "If—," "Gunga Din"; youngest Nobel literature laureate.
Victorian poet; Sonnets from the Portuguese ("How do I love thee?"), Aurora Leigh; married Robert Browning.
Scottish Victorian novelist; The Strange Case of Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde, Treasure Island, Kidnapped.
Victorian novelist of the Barsetshire and Palliser series; The Warden, Barchester Towers.
Thackeray's "novel without a hero" satirizing social climbing through the schemer Becky Sharp.
Victorian novelist and satirist; Vanity Fair, narrated by an intrusive authorial "Manager of the Performance."
A. E. Housman's 1896 poetry collection of terse, melancholy lyrics on youth, death, and rural England.
Victorian poet of Decadent, transgressive lyricism and classical meters; associated with the Pre-Raphaelites.
Arthur Conan Doyle's deductive detective of Baker Street, aided by Dr. Watson and foiled by Professor Moriarty.
Elizabeth Barrett Browning's love-sonnet sequence for Robert Browning, including "How do I love thee?"
Christina Rossetti's Pre-Raphaelite narrative poem of sisters Laura and Lizzie tempted by goblin merchants' fruit.
George Eliot's panoramic novel of provincial life, centered on idealistic Dorothea Brooke and doctor Lydgate.
Matthew Arnold's poem lamenting faith's "melancholy, long, withdrawing roar" as heard from a Dover shoreline.
Victorian/Georgian classical scholar-poet; A Shropshire Lad, terse lyrics on mortality and lost youth.
Dickens novel satirizing the interminable Chancery lawsuit Jarndyce and Jarndyce; narrated partly by Esther Summerson.
Victorian novelist; Jane Eyre and Villette, elder of the literary Bronte sisters.
Pre-Raphaelite-adjacent poet; "Goblin Market," devotional and love lyrics; sister of Dante Gabriel Rossetti.
Victorian "Condition of England" novelist; North and South, Mary Barton, biographer of Charlotte Bronte.
Dickens novel of orphan Pip, the escaped convict Magwitch, and the jilted Miss Havisham.
Pen name of Charles Dodgson; Alice's Adventures in Wonderland and Through the Looking-Glass.
Wilkie Collins's early detective novel about a stolen Indian diamond, often called the first English detective novel.
Victorian creator of Sherlock Holmes; also wrote historical fiction and The Lost World.
Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood founder, poet-painter; brother of Christina Rossetti.
Oscar Wilde's comedy of manners involving a fan, a scandal, and a mysterious Mrs. Erlynne.
Idealistic heroine of George Eliot's Middlemarch, unhappily married to the pedantic scholar Casaubon.

Thomas Hardy's Wessex novel of farmer Bathsheba Everdene and her three suitors, including shepherd Gabriel Oak.
Byronic, vengeful foundling of Emily Bronte's Wuthering Heights, obsessed with Catherine Earnshaw.
Elizabeth Gaskell's "Condition of England" novel contrasting industrial North and genteel South through Margaret Hale.
Oscar Wilde's farce of mistaken identities built on two men's fictitious "Ernest" personas.
Victorian sensation novelist; The Moonstone and The Woman in White; friend of Dickens.
Emily Bronte's novel of the destructive passion between Heathcliff and Catherine Earnshaw on the Yorkshire moors.
Dickens's novel set during the French Revolution, ending with Sydney Carton's self-sacrifice.
Dickens's semi-autobiographical novel following its orphaned narrator from childhood hardship to authorship.
Oscar Wilde's novel in which a portrait ages and bears the moral corruption of its eternally youthful subject.
Lewis Carroll's nonsense poem from Through the Looking-Glass, featuring the "vorpal sword" and "Tumtum tree."
Dante Gabriel Rossetti's Pre-Raphaelite poem of a dead woman watching for her lover from heaven.
Full name of poet A. E. Housman, author of A Shropshire Lad.
Youngest Bronte sister and novelist; Agnes Grey, The Tenant of Wildfell Hall.
Elizabeth Barrett Browning's verse novel about a woman poet's artistic and romantic development.
Ambitious, amoral social climber at the center of Thackeray's Vanity Fair.
Dickens's novel satirizing utilitarian education and industrial Coketown through Thomas Gradgrind.
Thomas Hardy's bleak final novel of stonemason Jude Fawley's thwarted ambitions and doomed love for Sue Bridehead.
Surname shared by Pre-Raphaelite poet-painter Dante Gabriel Rossetti and his poet sister Christina.
Victorian essayist and historian; Sartor Resartus and On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History.
Thomas Hardy's turn-of-the-century poem in which a thrush's song offers fragile hope amid bleak winter landscape.
Tennyson's poem of a cursed weaver who dies after leaving her tower to look directly at Sir Lancelot.
Gerard Manley Hopkins's sprung-rhythm elegy for nuns drowned in a shipwreck.
Matthew Arnold's pastoral elegy mourning his friend and fellow poet Arthur Hugh Clough.
Oscar Wilde's long prose letter written in prison, reflecting on his downfall and relationship with Lord Alfred Douglas.
Victorian novelist and playwright; Pelham, opened a novel with "It was a dark and stormy night."

entries.answerline: Middlemarch, A Study of Provincial Life
entries.note: Full title of George Eliot's panoramic novel of provincial English life.

variants.answerline: Middlemarch: A Study of Provincial Life

Prison where Oscar Wilde was incarcerated, subject of his poem "The Ballad of Reading Gaol."
George Eliot's novel of an embittered weaver redeemed by raising an abandoned child, Eppie.
Thomas Hardy's Wessex novel set on Egdon Heath, centered on Eustacia Vye's thwarted longing for escape.

entries.note: Robert Louis Stevenson's novella of a respectable doctor's potion-induced transformation into a violent alter ego.
Charlotte Bronte's novel of a reserved Englishwoman teaching at a girls' school in a fictional Belgian city.
Rudyard Kipling's advice poem to his son on stoic resilience, ending "you'll be a Man, my son!"
Thomas Hardy's poem on the Titanic disaster, framing iceberg and ship as fated for each other.
Tennyson's poem, based on the Odyssey episode, of sailors lulled into languid forgetfulness by a magical fruit.
Gerard Manley Hopkins's sonnet praising a kestrel's flight as a sprung-rhythm image of Christ.
George Eliot's debut novel of a carpenter's love and a seduction plot in rural England.

Bram Stoker's epistolary Gothic novel of the vampiric Count Dracula's threat to Victorian England.
Victorian novelist and poet; The Egoist, and the sonnet sequence Modern Love on a failing marriage.
Robert Louis Stevenson's adventure novel of young David Balfour's abduction and alliance with Jacobite Alan Breck.
George Meredith's sonnet sequence charting the disintegration of a marriage.

Oscar Wilde's play (first written in French) dramatizing the biblical princess's demand for John the Baptist's head.

entries.topic: Silas Marner: the Weaver of Raveloe
entries.answerline: Silas Marner: the Weaver of Raveloe
entries.note: Full title of George Eliot's novel of an embittered weaver redeemed by an adopted child.

entries.topic: Tess of the D'Urbervilles: A Pure Woman Faithfully Presented
entries.answerline: Tess of the D'Urbervilles: A Pure Woman Faithfully Presented
entries.note: Full title of Thomas Hardy's novel of a wronged milkmaid destroyed by Victorian sexual morality.
Thomas Hardy's novel of Michael Henchard, who sells his wife in youth and is later ruined by the past resurfacing.

entries.topic: Vanity Fair: A Novel without a Hero
entries.answerline: Vanity Fair: A Novel without a Hero
entries.note: Full subtitle of Thackeray's novel satirizing social climbing through the schemer Becky Sharp.
Gerard Manley Hopkins's sonnet praising God for all things "counter, original, spare, strange."
Oscar Wilde's dialogue-essay arguing that art should not imitate life but that "life imitates art."
W. W. Jacobs's horror story in which a cursed talisman grants wishes with terrible unintended consequences.
Matthew Arnold's poem on an Oxford student who abandons society to wander with gypsies in pursuit of secret knowledge.
Tennyson's close friend, whose early death inspired the elegy In Memoriam A.H.H.
Victorian prime minister and novelist; Sybil, or The Two Nations, on class division in industrial England.
Edward Rochester's hidden, mad first wife, confined in the attic in Charlotte Bronte's Jane Eyre.
Sherlock Holmes's loyal companion and the narrator of most of Arthur Conan Doyle's stories.
Poetic form perfected by Robert Browning, in which a single speaker reveals character unwittingly.
Victorian nonsense poet and illustrator; limericks and "The Owl and the Pussy-Cat."

Jilted, embittered recluse in Dickens's Great Expectations who raises Estella to break men's hearts.
Dickens's novel of an orphan drawn into a London den of thieves led by Fagin.

Becky Sharp's dashing but foolish husband in Thackeray's Vanity Fair.

Unfinished, ultimately futile scholarly project of the pedantic Reverend Casaubon in Eliot's Middlemarch.
George Eliot's novel of Maggie Tulliver's conflict between desire and duty in provincial England.
Dickens's episodic debut novel following the genial Mr. Pickwick and his club on their travels.
Anne Bronte's novel of a woman who flees an abusive, dissolute husband to protect her son.

entries.topic: Through the Looking-Glass, and What Alice Found There
entries.answerline: Through the Looking-Glass, and What Alice Found There
entries.note: Full title of Lewis Carroll's sequel to Alice's Adventures in Wonderland.
Genteel Linton family estate contrasted with Wuthering Heights in Emily Bronte's novel.
Surname of Victorian novelist Anthony Trollope, author of the Barsetshire and Palliser novels.
Fictional region of southwest England invented by Thomas Hardy as the setting for his novels.
Victorian poet; "Invictus," written from a hospital bed, declaring "I am the master of my fate."
Gerard Manley Hopkins's sonnet elegizing a dying blacksmith parishioner.
Kipling's poem honoring a heroic Indian water-carrier who serves British soldiers despite their scorn.
Thomas Hardy's poem on the indifferent, blind chance ("Crass Casualty") governing human suffering.
W. E. Henley's poem declaring "I am the master of my fate: I am the captain of my soul."

Oscar Wilde's poem written after his imprisonment, mourning a fellow inmate hanged for murder.
Tennyson's poem honoring the doomed cavalry charge into the "valley of Death" at Balaclava.

Algernon Charles Swinburne's classically styled verse drama on the Greek huntress Atalanta.
Anthony Trollope's novel of ecclesiastical rivalry and comedy in the fictional cathedral town of Barchester.

Sheridan Le Fanu's novella of a female vampire preying on a young woman, predating Dracula.

entries.topic: The Playboy of the Western World
entries.answerline: The Playboy of the Western World
entries.note: J. M. Synge's Irish Literary Revival play whose hero Christy Mahon is lionized for supposedly killing his father.

Virginia Woolf's essay arguing a woman needs money and "a room of her own" to write fiction.
Modernist novelist; Brave New World, a dystopia of engineered contentment and the drug soma.
James Joyce's short-story collection depicting paralysis and epiphany in early-20th-century Dublin life.

Virginia Woolf's modernist novel centered on the Ramsay family's deferred visit to a Hebridean lighthouse.

Virginia Woolf's novel following a nobleman who lives centuries and changes sex to a woman partway through.
Shorthand for Welsh modernist poet Dylan Thomas.
T. S. Eliot's fragmented modernist poem of postwar spiritual desolation, ending "Shantih shantih shantih."
Aldous Huxley's dystopia of engineered caste and the pacifying drug soma, with outsider John the Savage.
Full name of novelist E. M. Forster.

Edwardian essayist and novelist; the Father Brown mysteries, The Man Who Was Thursday.

Full name of novelist and playwright W. Somerset Maugham.
E. M. Forster's novel of Lucy Honeychurch's awakening in Florence and choice between suitors.
Joseph Conrad's novella of Marlow's journey up the Congo to find the ivory trader Kurtz.

J. M. Synge's one-act tragedy of an Aran Islands mother who loses her sons to the sea.
Irish playwright of Dublin tenement life; Juno and the Paycock, The Plough and the Stars.
D. H. Lawrence's semi-autobiographical novel of Paul Morel's stifling attachment to his mother.
Ford Madox Ford's unreliable-narrator novel unraveling the hidden affairs behind two "good" couples.
Full name of modernist poet T. S. Eliot.
T. S. Eliot's dramatic monologue of an old man reflecting on history and spiritual emptiness.
Full name of Welsh poet Dylan Thomas.
Full name of Edwardian essayist and novelist G. K. Chesterton.
Sean O'Casey's tragicomedy of a Dublin tenement family amid the Irish Civil War.
Joseph Conrad's novel of a disgraced sailor seeking redemption after abandoning a sinking ship.
Virginia Woolf's novel of a nobleman who lives for centuries and changes sex, dedicated to Vita Sackville-West.
Dylan Thomas's radio play giving voice to the residents of a fictional Welsh fishing village over one day.
Wilfred Owen's World War I poem condemning the "old Lie" that dying for one's country is sweet and fitting.
Dylan Thomas's nostalgic poem recalling an idyllic Welsh farm childhood before the shadow of mortality.
Yeats's late poem admitting his poetic imagery must now begin "in the foul rag-and-bone shop of the heart."
Yeats's apocalyptic poem declaring that "the centre cannot hold" as a "rough beast" slouches toward Bethlehem.
E. M. Forster's novel of a fraught friendship strained by a disputed incident in the Marabar Caves.
Interwar novelist of Weimar Berlin; Goodbye to Berlin, source for the musical Cabaret.
Anglo-Irish modernist novelist; The Death of the Heart, known for psychological precision.
Edwardian pioneer of science fiction; The Time Machine, The War of the Worlds, The Island of Doctor Moreau.
David Jones's modernist long poem blending prose and verse to depict World War I trench experience.
J. M. Barrie's Edwardian play and novel of the boy who refuses to grow up, ruler of Neverland.
World War I poet known for idealistic patriotism; "The Soldier," killed in 1915 before seeing major combat.
G. K. Chesterton's surreal thriller in which anarchist infiltrators turn out to be undercover detectives.
Sean O'Casey's play set among Dublin tenement dwellers during the 1916 Easter Rising.
Bloomsbury-adjacent writer and Virginia Woolf's lover, the inspiration for Woolf's Orlando.
T. S. Eliot's poem of religious conversion and penitence, written after he joined the Anglican Church.

entries.topic: "Do Not Go Gentle Into That Good Night"
entries.answerline: "Do Not Go Gentle Into That Good Night"
entries.note: Dylan Thomas's villanelle urging his dying father to "rage against the dying of the light."
Yeats's poem on the Irish Easter Rising, repeating that a "terrible beauty is born."
Yeats's poem of an aging man seeking artistic immortality in the Byzantine capital, "no country for old men."
T. S. Eliot's poem of spiritual emptiness ending "not with a bang but a whimper."
Saki's short story of feuding landowners trapped under a fallen tree, ironically reconciled just before death.
Recurring narrator of Joseph Conrad's fiction, including Heart of Darkness and Lord Jim.
Failed, vain poet in Max Beerbohm's story who sells his soul to glimpse his posthumous (nonexistent) fame.

Edwardian-era novelist and Nobel laureate; The Forsyte Saga, chronicling an upper-middle-class family.
Outsider raised outside the World State in Huxley's Brave New World, ultimately destroyed by its values.
E. M. Forster's novel of homosexual love in Edwardian England, published only after his death.
Comic novelist creator of valet Jeeves and hapless Bertie Wooster.
W. Somerset Maugham's novel loosely based on Gauguin, of a stockbroker who abandons his life to paint.
Full name of English-born modernist poet W. H. Auden.
D. H. Lawrence short story of a miner's wife awaiting her husband, who is brought home dead from an accident.
Title shared by Katherine Mansfield's story of a privileged girl confronting death nearby, and Vaclav Havel's Absurdist play.
Full name of Brave New World author Aldous Huxley.
Circle of early-20th-century writers and artists including Virginia Woolf and Lytton Strachey.
Lytton Strachey's irreverent group biography debunking four celebrated Victorian figures.
T. S. Eliot's four-part meditative poem sequence on time, memory, and spiritual renewal.
Robert Graves's memoir of his World War I trench experience and disillusioned break from English society.

Joseph Conrad's novel of political corruption and a stolen silver hoard in a fictional South American country.

Clubfooted protagonist of Maugham's semi-autobiographical novel Of Human Bondage.

Max Beerbohm's comic novel of an irresistible beauty whose Oxford visit causes mass undergraduate suicide.
Yeats's poem, written while touring a schoolroom, asking "how can we know the dancer from the dance?"
Dylan Thomas's poem defiantly asserting that death holds no ultimate power over the dead.

T. S. Eliot's early poem depicting a stifling, one-sided drawing-room relationship.
Saki's dark short story of a sickly boy who worships a ferret as a vengeful god against his guardian.
Alfred Noyes's narrative poem of a doomed romance between a highwayman and an innkeeper's daughter.

entries.topic: "The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock"
entries.answerline: "The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock"
entries.note: T. S. Eliot's poem of a hesitant, self-doubting speaker who dares not "disturb the universe."
Joseph Conrad's novella of a young ship captain secretly harboring a fugitive who resembles his double.
Recurring, house-haunting refrain in D. H. Lawrence's "The Rocking-Horse Winner."
Edwardian novelist; The Old Wives' Tale, chronicling provincial Staffordshire life.
Urban squalor imagery characteristic of T. S. Eliot's early modernist poetry.

Ancient city Yeats imagines as an eternal artistic refuge in "Sailing to Byzantium."
Ford Madox Ford's protagonist in Parade's End, an old-fashioned gentleman undone by war and infidelity.
Aldous Huxley's debut novel, a satirical country-house comedy of intellectual pretension.

entries.topic: dulce et decorum est pro patria mori
entries.answerline: dulce et decorum est pro patria mori
entries.note: Latin tag, "sweet and fitting to die for one's country," bitterly condemned as "the old Lie" in Wilfred Owen's poem.
G. K. Chesterton's unassuming priest-detective who solves crimes through moral insight.
Full name of science-fiction pioneer H. G. Wells.
Ezra Pound's poem sequence reflecting on art, war, and his own poetic career.
Robert Graves's historical novel narrated by the unassuming Roman emperor Claudius.
Hesitant, self-doubting speaker of T. S. Eliot's "The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock."
Full name of Irish modernist novelist James Joyce.

Full name of World War I poet Siegfried Sassoon.

Joseph Conrad's novel of a Russian student's betrayal and moral reckoning set partly in Geneva.
Nineteen-line poetic form with repeating refrains, used by Dylan Thomas in "Do Not Go Gentle into That Good Night."
Full name of World War I trench poet Wilfred Owen.

sections.name: Mid-20th-Century Fiction, Poetry & Theatre
sections.blurb: Postwar writing spanning dystopian satire, the Movement, Absurdist and kitchen-sink drama, and Catholic novelists.

entries.note: Irish-born Fabian socialist playwright; Pygmalion, Major Barbara, Saint Joan; witty prose prefaces on social reform.

Poet of The Movement known for plain-spoken melancholy; "This Be The Verse," "An Arundel Tomb," The Whitsun Weddings.
Catholic novelist of moral ambiguity and espionage; The Power and the Glory, The Heart of the Matter, The Quiet American.
Playwright of witty, intellectual drama; Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead, Arcadia, Travesties.
Satirical Catholic novelist; Brideshead Revisited, Vile Bodies, Scoop, A Handful of Dust.

Harold Pinter's 1957 "comedy of menace" set in a seaside boarding house, with intruders Goldberg and McCann.
Tom Stoppard's play interleaving 1809 and modern-day scholars at an English country house, exploring chaos and thermodynamics.

20th-century Gothic novelist; Rebecca, with its unnamed narrator and sinister Manderley; "The Birds."
Noel Coward's comedy in which a novelist's seance summons his dead first wife's ghost to haunt his second marriage.

entries.topic: The Prime of Miss Jean Brodie
entries.answerline: The Prime of Miss Jean Brodie

Samuel Beckett's absurdist play in which Vladimir and Estragon wait futilely for a figure who never arrives.
Anthony Burgess's novel of ultraviolent delinquent Alex, narrated in the invented slang Nadsat.

Daphne du Maurier's story of a Cornish farming community besieged by inexplicably aggressive birds.
Evelyn Waugh's satirical novel ending with its betrayed protagonist trapped reading Dickens aloud in the Amazon.
Sylvia Plath's posthumously published poetry collection, edited by her husband Ted Hughes.
Flann O'Brien's metafictional comic novel in which characters rebel against their student-author.

Mid-20th-century novelist; The Once and Future King, a retelling of the King Arthur legend.
Graham Greene's novel of a guilt-ridden colonial policeman torn between duty, adultery, and Catholic faith.

Iris Murdoch's Booker-winning novel of a retired theater director obsessed with a childhood love.

William Golding's novel following a drowning sailor's desperate consciousness clinging to survival on a rock.

Alan Sillitoe's Angry Young Men-era novel of a rebellious young factory worker's affairs and defiance.
