entries.note: Nation whose literary tradition — Swift, Wilde, Yeats, Joyce, Beckett, Heaney — looms large within "British Literature."
Capital and recurring setting across British literature, from Dickens's serialized city to Zadie Smith's multicultural novels.
Nation contributing Burns, Scott, Stevenson, and Muriel Spark to the British literary canon.
Conflict that shaped the war poetry of Owen, Sassoon, and Brooke and the modernist break from Victorian certainty.
Irish capital and setting central to Joyce's Dubliners and Ulysses.
Gothic figures central to Stoker's Dracula and Le Fanu's Carmilla within British Gothic fiction.
Roman lyric poet and satirist frequently invoked as a classical model for English poetry.
Recurring thematic preoccupation, from Sterne's digressions to Eliot's Four Quartets and Woolf's fiction.
Nation contributing Atwood and Munro to the broader English-language literary tradition.
Medieval Italian poet of the Divine Comedy, a recurring influence and reference point for English poets.
Fourteen-line poetic form used across British literature, most famously by Shakespeare, Donne, and Wordsworth.
Recurring image, notably in Yeats's "Leda and the Swan" and "The Wild Swans at Coole."
Recurring servant figure, epitomized by Stevens in Ishiguro's The Remains of the Day and Jeeves in Wodehouse.
Nation contributing Dylan Thomas and other Welsh voices to the British literary canon.

entries.topic: Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom
entries.answerline: Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom
entries.note: Honorary royal appointment held over time by Dryden, Wordsworth, Tennyson, Hughes, and Duffy, among others.
Common surname shared by British writers including novelist Zadie Smith and poet Stevie Smith.
American-born novelist of psychological realism who became a British subject; The Portrait of a Lady, The Ambassadors.
Shorthand for American-born, British-naturalized novelist Henry James.
Universal theme running through British literature from Donne's lyrics to Austen's marriage plots.
Recurring artist-figure archetype across British fiction and poetry.
Recurring genre figure, central to Graham Greene's and John le Carre's Cold War-era fiction.
Roman comic playwright frequently cited as a classical antecedent of English comedy.
Recurring setting and symbol, from Conrad's sailor fiction to Synge's Riders to the Sea and Murdoch's The Sea, The Sea.
Roman epic poet of the Aeneid, a recurring classical model and guide-figure in English literature.
Shorthand for Welsh poet Dylan Thomas.
Common name shared by figures from Elizabeth I to Austen's heroine Elizabeth Bennet.
Recurring theological presence, central to Milton's theodicy in Paradise Lost and Donne's devotional poetry.
Genre of supernatural dread and ruined settings spanning Walpole and Radcliffe to Shelley's Frankenstein and du Maurier.
Epistolary form used across British fiction, from Richardson's Pamela and Clarissa to Bram Stoker's Dracula.
Recurring mood across British literature, from Hamlet's brooding to Keats's odes and Larkin's poetry.
Recurring animal figure, most notably the ruling swine of Orwell's Animal Farm.
Shorthand for Irish Nobel poet Seamus Heaney.
Recurring celestial image across British poetry, from Romantic nature verse to Gothic atmosphere.
Initial shared by British literary figures such as Auden, Wordsworth, and Wilde.
Recurring domestic role and source of conflict across British drama and the novel of manners.
Recurring predatory image in British poetry and fable, and namesake of Mantel's Wolf Hall.
Commonwealth nation contributing writers like Peter Carey and Patrick White to the broader English canon.
Narrative folk-song form revived by Wordsworth and Coleridge's Lyrical Ballads.
1815 battle ending the Napoleonic Wars, referenced across 19th-century British literature.
Recurring aesthetic theme, central to Keats's odes and Wilde's Aestheticism.
Comic motif of Flann O'Brien's The Third Policeman, whose "atomic theory" merges men and bicycles.
Subject of T. S. Eliot's whimsical Old Possum's Book of Practical Cats, basis for the musical Cats.
Initial shared by literary figures such as Dickens, Donne, and Defoe.
Personified or thematic presence across British literature, from Everyman to Donne's "Death, be not proud."
Nation whose shifting national identity and landscape recur across the British literary canon.
Rural setting central to Orwell's allegorical Animal Farm and Hardy's Wessex novels.
Supernatural figures central to Gothic fiction and, in Ibsen's play Ghosts, a non-British touchstone for the theme.
Genre of supernatural dread and ruined settings spanning Walpole and Radcliffe to Shelley and du Maurier.
Classical Greek figure whose beauty and abduction recur as a literary allusion across English poetry.
Setting and subject of empire-era and postcolonial writing, from Kipling to Forster and Rushdie.
Initial shared by literary figures such as Joyce, Johnson, and Jonson.
Common heroine name, notably Winston Smith's lover in Orwell's Nineteen Eighty-Four.
Classical language underlying English literary education and translated by figures like Dryden and Pope.
University city and setting recurring across British literature, home to numerous authors and campus novels.
Ancient university educating a wide swath of English writers, from Sidney to Auden and Tolkien.
Recurring symbol, from the Wars of the Roses to Blake's "The Sick Rose" and Shakespeare's "a rose by any other name."
Spiritualist ritual comically deployed in Noel Coward's Blithe Spirit to summon a ghostly ex-wife.
Pastoral figure recurring from Marlowe's "Passionate Shepherd" to Milton's "Lycidas" and Hardy's Wessex.
Ancient Greek originator of pastoral poetry, a model for English pastoral verse.
Roman comic playwright whose stock characters influenced English comedy of manners.
Recurring plot resolution across British comedy, from Shakespeare's comedies to Austen's novels.
Conflict shaping mid-20th-century British writing, from Waugh's satire to Greene's espionage fiction.
Curious child-heroine of Lewis Carroll's Alice's Adventures in Wonderland.
Canadian Nobel-winning short-story writer, associated with the broader English-language literary tradition.
Common heroine name, including Austen's Anne Elliot in Persuasion.
Recurring Gothic-novel setting, from Walpole's The Castle of Otranto to Radcliffe's romances.
Recurring beloved's name in English lyric poetry, including Ben Jonson's "Drink to me only with thine eyes."
Dramatic and narrative mode spanning Shakespeare's comedies to Restoration comedy of manners.
Central image of Wordsworth's "I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud."
1916 Irish rebellion against British rule, commemorated in Yeats's poem "Easter, 1916" and O'Casey's plays.
Recurring symbolic setting, from Milton's Eden to Mansfield's "The Garden Party" and English pastoral verse.
Genre of supernatural dread and ruined settings spanning Walpole and Radcliffe to Shelley and du Maurier.
Recurring Victorian occupation and plot device, exemplified by Jane Eyre.
Recurring color motif, notably the girdle and knight in Sir Gawain and the Green Knight.
Ancient Greek epic poet of the Iliad and Odyssey, translated by Pope and Chapman and echoed in Joyce's Ulysses.
Frequent setting for British fiction, from Forster's A Room with a View to Byron's and Shelley's exile.
American film director (Stagecoach, The Searchers), distinct from the Jacobean playwright of the same name.
Symbolic figure of innocence, most famously in Blake's "The Lamb" from Songs of Innocence.
Mythological woman ravished by Zeus as a swan, subject of Yeats's poem "Leda and the Swan."
Setting of Malcolm Lowry's novel Under the Volcano, among other British literary works.
Home nation of modernist short-story writer Katherine Mansfield.
Historic London prison referenced across Dickens's novels and the "Newgate novel" crime genre.
Recurring symbolic bird, central to Keats's "Ode to a Nightingale."
Recurring subject, notably in De Quincey's Confessions of an English Opium-Eater and Coleridge's "Kubla Khan."
Ancient Greek lyric poet of victory odes, a model for English Pindaric odes like Cowley's.
Recurring setting, from Wilde's Reading Gaol to Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress and Dickens's debtors' prisons.
Driving theme of Jacobean tragedy, from Hamlet to The Duchess of Malfi and The Revenger's Tragedy.
Common name shared by Shakespeare's kings Richard II and Richard III.
Faith central to the fiction of Waugh, Greene, and Muriel Spark, often called Catholic literature.
Recurring pastoral figures across English poetry, from Marlowe and Spenser to Hardy's Wessex novels.
World War II bombing campaign against Britain, a recurring backdrop in mid-20th-century fiction.
Recurring setting for mystery and travel narratives, as in Christie's Murder on the Orient Express.
Ancient Greek prose romance by Heliodorus, an influential model for later English romance fiction.
Recurring figure of self-reflection, especially in Joyce's A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man.
Georgian spa city setting central to Jane Austen's Northanger Abbey and Persuasion.
Surname shared by wartime statesman and Nobel literature laureate Winston Churchill and playwright Caryl Churchill.
Southwestern English setting for du Maurier's Rebecca and Jamaica Inn.
Recurring social ritual in the novel of manners, from Austen's balls to Woolf's parties.
Word for growing darkness used in Hardy's "The Darkling Thrush" and Arnold's "Dover Beach."
Dutch Renaissance humanist and friend of Thomas More; In Praise of Folly, influential on English humanism.
Recurring genre figure, from Sherlock Holmes to Agatha Christie's Poirot.
Initial shared by literary figures such as Eliot and Edgeworth.
Folk narrative genre reworked by Angela Carter in The Bloody Chamber.
Upheaval that shaped Wordsworth's early idealism and the setting of Dickens's A Tale of Two Cities.
Recurring clerical figure, including Friar Laurence in Romeo and Juliet.
Roman lyric poet whose personal, passionate verse influenced English love poetry.
Recurring symbolic setting, from Milton's Eden to Mansfield's "The Garden Party" and English pastoral verse.
Major Scottish city and setting recurring in Scottish literary fiction.
French realist novelist of Madame Bovary, a touchstone for the development of the English novel.
Common royal name shared by several Shakespeare history-play kings.
Devotional song form written by figures such as William Cowper and John Newton.
National identifier shared by a large share of "British Literature" answerlines, from Swift to Heaney.
Birth country of Nobel laureate Kazuo Ishiguro, who moved to England as a child.
Central figure of Christian devotional poetry, from Donne's Holy Sonnets to Hopkins's verse.
Roman satirist whose biting verse satires influenced the English satiric tradition.
Common heroine name, including Gaskell's Margaret Hale in North and South.
French libertine writer whose name and transgressive themes recur as a comparison point in Gothic fiction.
French comic playwright whose comedy of manners influenced Restoration English theater.
Recurring philosophical motif, echoing Lear's "Nothing will come of nothing" in Shakespeare's tragedy.
Australian novelist and two-time Booker Prize winner, associated with the broader Commonwealth literary tradition.
Formal poetic device varying widely across English verse, from Chaucer's rhyme royal to blank-verse Milton's rejection of it.
Recurring natural image, from the Thames in Dickens to Wordsworth's "Tintern Abbey" on the River Wye.
Dialect used by Robert Burns and other Scottish poets alongside standard English.
Mediterranean setting recurring in Shakespeare's romances, including The Winter's Tale.
Recurring theme in colonial and postcolonial British literature, including Behn's Oroonoko.
Symptom of guilt dramatized in Lady Macbeth's famous sleepwalking scene.
1930s conflict that shaped Orwell's Homage to Catalonia and 1930s British poetry.
Recurring celestial image across British poetry, from Sidney's Astrophil and Stella to Hardy's fatalism.
Recurring emotional motif across British poetry and the Victorian sentimental novel.
Recurring celestial image across British poetry, from Donne's "The Sun Rising" to Blake's symbolism.
Speculative device introduced to English fiction chiefly through H. G. Wells's The Time Machine.
Recurring natural image across English pastoral and Romantic poetry.
Nation whose literary tradition, spanning England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland, forms this unit.
Nation of birth for several writers, such as T. S. Eliot and Henry James, later naturalized British.
Recurring setting of the campus novel, from Kingsley Amis's Lucky Jim to David Lodge's fiction.
National identifier for writers including Dylan Thomas within the British literary tradition.
Prized literary quality central to Restoration comedy of manners and Wilde's epigrammatic style.
Prophetic, supernatural figures who set Macbeth's tragedy in motion in Shakespeare's play.
Recurring predatory image, echoed in the title of Mantel's Wolf Hall.
Decade encompassing Edwardian and early-modernist literature, framed by World War I.
Initial shared by literary figures such as Austen, Arnold, and Auden.
Recurring emotional theme, notably in Osborne's Look Back in Anger and the Angry Young Men movement.
Colonial American Puritan poet, an early figure in the broader English-language poetic tradition.
American formalist poet, part of the wider English-language poetic tradition rather than the British canon proper.
Sophoclean Greek tragic heroine who defies the state to bury her brother, a recurring classical touchstone.
American modernist poet, part of the wider English-language poetic tradition rather than the British canon proper.
Recurring natural image across English pastoral and nature poetry.
Recurring theme of aging, from Browning's "Rabbi Ben Ezra" to Yeats's late poetry.
English seaside town setting of Greene's Brighton Rock.
Novel by Portuguese Nobel laureate Jose Saramago reimagining the biblical fratricide, a non-British comparanda.
Common name shared by literary figures and characters, including Dickens and Marlow-narrated protagonists.
Recurring subject across British literature, from Dickens's orphans to children's authors like Carroll and Dahl.
Recurring festive setting, most famously in Dickens's A Christmas Carol.
Recurring setting, notably in Larkin's poem "Church Going."
English sport recurring as a cultural touchstone across British fiction and essay writing.
Recurring bird image, often symbolizing displacement or cuckoldry in English poetry.
Choderlos de Laclos's French epistolary novel of aristocratic seduction and manipulation, a non-British touchstone.
Recurring symbolic motif, central to Conrad's Heart of Darkness and Milton's fallen angels.
Recurring professional figure, from Marlowe's Faustus to Conan Doyle's Watson and Stevenson's Jekyll.
Recurring literary device, from medieval dream-visions like Piers Plowman to Gothic and Romantic fiction.
Recurring fate across British literature, including Ophelia in Hamlet and Milton's "Lycidas."
Recurring heraldic and natural image across British poetry and myth.
Christian feast recurring across British literature, notably in Yeats's poem "Easter, 1916."
Literary device describing a work of visual art, as in Keats's "Ode on a Grecian Urn."
American confessional-adjacent poet, part of the wider English-language poetic tradition rather than the British canon.
Fantasy race populating Tolkien's Middle-earth legendarium.
Commemorative verse form, notably central to Gray's "Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard."
Italian city setting of Forster's A Room with a View.
Recurring symbolic motif across English pastoral and Romantic poetry.
Recurring number, as in Eliot's Four Quartets and Chesterton's four investigators-turned-detectives motif.
Recurring animal figure in English fable and hunting-themed literature.
Medieval French poet of vagabond life and mortality, an influence on and point of comparison for English verse.
Title creatures of Aristophanes's ancient Greek comedy, a non-British classical touchstone.
Italian Renaissance poet of passionate love sonnets, a continental counterpart to English sonneteers.
Italian Romantic poet of pessimistic lyric verse, a continental counterpart to English Romanticism.
Medieval Italian author of the Decameron, a major influence on Chaucer's Canterbury Tales.
American horror writer whose cosmic dread expands on the British Gothic tradition.
Recurring motif, notably the stolen lock in Pope's mock-epic The Rape of the Lock.
Recurring theological setting, contrasted with Milton's Hell and central to devotional poetry.
Recurring value at stake in Restoration comedy and Renaissance tragedy alike.
Recurring animal presence, notably the rational Houyhnhnms of Swift's Gulliver's Travels.
Key word of Oscar Wilde's farce The Importance of Being Earnest, built on double identities.
Erasmus's satirical essay, narrated by personified Folly, mocking clerical and scholarly pretension.
First part of Dante's Divine Comedy, depicting a journey through Hell, a recurring reference point in English poetry.
Recurring thematic contrast, notably in Blake's Songs of Innocence and of Experience.
Early-2000s conflict addressed in contemporary British fiction and political writing.
Athol Fugard's South African play on political imprisonment under apartheid, a non-British comparanda.
Recurring setting, from Swift's Lilliput to Shakespeare's Tempest and Stevenson's Treasure Island.
Common heroine name recurring across English fiction and verse.
Recurring subject of English literary treatment, from Shakespeare's Shylock to Eliot's Daniel Deronda.
Common name shared by numerous British literary figures, including Donne, Milton, and Keats.
Russian-American Nobel laureate poet, part of the broader English-language literary conversation.
Recurring philosophical subject across British poetry, drama, and the novel.
Ancient Greek or Roman rhetorician traditionally credited with On the Sublime, influential on English literary criticism.
British prime minister whose era shapes the political backdrop of Churchill's Top Girls and other contemporary fiction.
Common heroine name, including Faust's beloved Gretchen (Marguerite) in Goethe's drama.
Euripides's Greek tragic sorceress who kills her own children in revenge against Jason, a recurring classical touchstone.
Recurring industrial image, from Eliot's The Mill on the Floss to Blake's "dark Satanic mills."
Recurring crime driving both Jacobean revenge tragedy and the modern detective novel.
Recurring aesthetic concern, from Milton's "L'Allegro" to Eliot's Four Quartets.
American setting occasionally featured in British fiction concerning transatlantic characters.
Recurring number in English literary tradition, echoing the Muses and various numerological schemes.
Jean Genet's transgressive French novel, a non-British touchstone for outsider and prison literature.
Recurring artist-figure archetype across British fiction and poetry.
Influential British publisher that popularized affordable paperback editions of literary works.
Mythological self-resurrecting bird recurring as a symbol of renewal in English poetry.
Recurring adventure-fiction figure, notably in Stevenson's Treasure Island.
Greek Titan who steals fire for humanity, a myth reworked in Shelley's Prometheus Unbound and Frankenstein's subtitle.
Full name of Roman epic poet Virgil, author of the Aeneid.
Recurring social setting in British fiction, from Dickens's inns to contemporary urban novels.
Recurring atmospheric motif across English pastoral and modernist poetry.
Nation whose literary tradition contributes heavily to the "British Literature" canon.
American poet of rural New England verse, part of the wider English-language poetic tradition.
Recurring symbol, from Blake's "The Sick Rose" to Shakespeare's "a rose by any other name."
National identifier for non-British authors occasionally referenced in comparison within British literature questions.
Classical Indian language occasionally referenced in postcolonial and Indian-influenced English literature.
Ancient Greek lyric poet of passionate, fragmentary verse, a touchstone for English lyric poetry.
Common heroine name recurring across English fiction and drama.
Roman genre of moral and social critique, exemplified by Juvenal and Horace.
Roman elegiac poet of passionate love verse, translated and adapted by Ezra Pound.
Recurring plot device, from Shakespeare's Tempest to Defoe's Robinson Crusoe.
Austrian psychoanalyst whose theories of the unconscious deeply influenced modernist literature.
Setting recurring in English literature, including Kyd's The Spanish Tragedy and Byron's exile.
Ancient stone monument referenced as a symbolic setting, including in Hardy's Tess of the d'Urbervilles.
Recurring seasonal motif, notably in Shakespeare's Sonnet 18 and A Midsummer Night's Dream.
Recurring occupational figure, notably in Spark's The Prime of Miss Jean Brodie.
Term for the Australian outback, recurring in Commonwealth literature set there.
Roman comedy by Terence (Heauton Timorumenos), a classical model for later English comic drama.
Recurring theological and philosophical subject, from Donne's devotional poetry to Milton's epics.
Classical conflict of Homeric epic, a recurring source of allusion in English poetry.
American poet known for introspective, nature-infused verse, part of the wider English-language poetic tradition.
Romanian-French founder of Dada, portrayed as a character in Stoppard's Travesties, a non-British figure.

entries.answerline: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland

Central European city setting, including Shakespeare's Measure for Measure.
Surname of Patrick White, Australian Nobel laureate novelist, part of the broader Commonwealth literary tradition.
Polish Nobel laureate poet, part of the broader international poetic tradition rather than the British canon.
